era_context: The late Qin Dynasty in China under Qin Shi Huang's reign (221–210 BCE). The Hellenistic world following Alexander's death (323 BCE), with the Seleucid Empire, Ptolemaic Egypt, and Macedonian kingdoms in flux. Rome is consolidating power in Italy and the Mediterranean following the Second Punic War (218–201 BCE), which ended in 201 BCE. The year 210 BCE marks the death of Qin Shi Huang and the beginning of succession instability that would lead to the Qin collapse within 15 years.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records for this period)
Mesoamerica (Maya and other civilisations; no direct dating to this year known)

events.title: Death of Qin Shi Huang
events.description: Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of unified China and founder of the Qin Dynasty, died in 210 BCE. His death triggered a succession crisis. According to the Shiji, he died during a tour of eastern China. His death was followed by court intrigue orchestrated by the eunuch Zhao Gao and minister Li Si, who manipulated the succession to place the younger son Huhai (later known as Qin Er Shi) on the throne instead of the intended heir Fusu. This succession dispute weakened central authority and contributed directly to the peasant rebellions and civil wars that would destroy the Qin within 15 years.
key_figures: Huhai / Qin Er Shi (successor, second emperor)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109–91 BCE
Qin administrative records and bamboo slips from archaeological sites

events.certainty_note: The death of Qin Shi Huang in 210 BCE is well-attested in the Shiji and supported by archaeological evidence of administrative transitions. The exact date (month/day) is less certain, but the year is secure.

events.title: Succession of Qin Er Shi (Huhai) as Second Emperor of Qin
events.description: Following Qin Shi Huang's death, the eunuch Zhao Gao and minister Li Si conspired to place Huhai, the younger son, on the throne instead of Fusu, the intended heir. Fusu was forced to commit suicide. Huhai became the second emperor, known as Qin Er Shi. He was weak and easily manipulated by Zhao Gao, who gradually consolidated power. This succession was illegitimate by the original imperial will and is portrayed in later sources as a turning point toward Qin's decline. The new emperor lacked the authority and capability of his father, and his reign saw increasing administrative dysfunction.
events.certainty_note: The succession of Qin Er Shi in 210 BCE is well-documented in the Shiji. The details of the conspiracy are attested in later sources but may reflect historiographic bias against the eunuch Zhao Gao.
cross_references: Follows from -210-001 (death of Qin Shi Huang)

events.title: Continued Hellenistic Warfare in the Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: In 210 BCE, the Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III (r. 223–187 BCE) was engaged in military campaigns to recover territories lost after Alexander's death. Antiochus III was consolidating Seleucid power in Asia Minor and the Levant. Simultaneously, Ptolemaic Egypt under Ptolemy IV Philopator (r. 221–204 BCE) was defending against Seleucid expansion. The Fourth Syrian War (219–217 BCE) had recently concluded with the Battle of Raphia (217 BCE), a Ptolemaic victory. In 210 BCE, the region remained in a state of strategic tension and intermittent conflict, with both powers seeking to dominate the eastern Mediterranean and control trade routes.

sources.name: Polybius, Histories (written ~150 BCE, covering events from 220 BCE onward)

events.certainty_note: The broader geopolitical situation in 210 BCE is well-attested, but specific events for this exact year are not detailed in surviving sources. The characterization is based on the known trajectory of Antiochus III's reign and Ptolemaic-Seleucid relations.
cross_references: Part of broader Hellenistic conflicts following -323-001 (Alexander's death)

events.title: Roman Consolidation in Italy and Mediterranean Expansion
events.description: In 210 BCE, Rome was consolidating its dominance in Italy and the Mediterranean following the Second Punic War (218–201 BCE), which had ended in 201 BCE with Roman victory over Carthage. The year 210 BCE falls within the period of Roman expansion and reorganization of conquered territories. Rome was establishing itself as the dominant Mediterranean power, though the Third Punic War (149–146 BCE) was still decades away. The Roman Republic's military and administrative systems were being refined through these campaigns and territorial acquisitions.

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (written ~27 BCE–17 CE, covering earlier periods)

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Roman expansion in 210 BCE is well-established, but specific events for this year are not prominently recorded in surviving sources.
cross_references: Follows from -201-001 (end of Second Punic War)

disconfirming_evidence: The succession of Qin Er Shi is portrayed in the Shiji as a clear conspiracy by Zhao Gao and Li Si against the rightful heir Fusu. However, modern scholarship has questioned whether this narrative reflects actual events or represents later historiographic bias. Some scholars suggest that the succession may have been more complex, with multiple factions involved and less clear-cut villainy on Zhao Gao's part. The Shiji was compiled over a century after these events and may reflect Han Dynasty perspectives on the Qin collapse. Additionally, the characterization of Qin Er Shi as uniformly weak is challenged by some modern historians who note that early in his reign he attempted to maintain Qin policies and authority.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 210 BCE is heavily weighted toward China, where the Shiji provides a detailed (if later-compiled) narrative. The Hellenistic and Roman worlds are documented through later Greek and Latin historians (Polybius, Livy, Appian) who wrote 100+ years after these events. For China, the Shiji is the primary source, compiled by Sima Qian in the early Han Dynasty (~109–91 BCE), meaning it was written over a century after the events of 210 BCE. Archaeological evidence from Qin administrative sites provides some corroboration but is limited. The narrative is shaped by Han Dynasty perspectives on the Qin collapse and may reflect court biases. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, Mesoamerica, and Oceania have no known written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Death of Qin Shi Huang directly triggered the succession crisis and placement of Qin Er Shi
Weak succession and administrative dysfunction under Qin Er Shi contributed to peasant rebellions in 209 BCE
Hellenistic conflicts in the east and Roman consolidation in the west were occurring simultaneously
